note: 美国太空部队的约翰·汤普森中将在本周早些时候的TechCrunch会议：太空中谈到了与初创公司合作的重要性。 将军是太空与导弹系统中心的司令官，负责美国太空部队的卫星及其相关指挥与控制系统的研究，设计，开发和获取。 他的角色使他与一些最有雄心和创新的初创公司直接接触。  汤普森将军广泛谈到了与美国企业（尤其是初创企业）合作的重要性，他认为许多公司生产适合航天部队任务的创新产品。 他提供了有关创业公司如何与太空部队互动的技巧。  ExtraCrunch订户和TechCrunch会议：持太空票的人可以观看下面的30分钟采访。
note_en:
Lt. Gen. John Thompson of the United States Space Force spoke at TechCrunch Session: Space earlier this week on the importance of working with startups. The general is the Commander of the Space and Missiles Systems Center, where he oversees research, design, development, and acquisition of satellites and their associated command and control systems for the U.S. Space Force. His role puts him in direct contact with some of the most ambitious and innovative startups.
 Gen. Thompson spoke extensively on the importance of working with American business, specifically startups, where he sees many producing innovative products that fit within Space Force’s missions.  He gave tips on how startups can interact with Space Force.
  ExtraCrunch subscribers and TechCrunch Session: Space ticket holders can watch the 30 minute interview below.
title: 观看太空部队的中将约翰·汤普森（John Thompson）谈采购技术以确保太空安全
title_en: Watch Space Force’s Lt. Gen. John Thompson speak on sourcing tech to secure space

note:
您可能会认识到Hooch的Tap Network名称及其联合创始人兼首席执行官Lin Dai，这家初创公司提供一日饮料订阅服务，然后将重点转移到更广泛的奖励计划上。戴告诉我，他“从Hooch的经验中学到了很多东西”，但最终“确定Tap的机会更大，我们实际上是在寻找回报。”
 因此，Tap Network是一家新成立的初创公司，最近刚刚从Revelis Capital，Nima Capital，福布斯家族办公室，华纳音乐集团，Access Industries，Bill Tai，Bob Hurst，Edward Devlin等投资者那里筹集了400万美元资金。
  戴说，正常的奖励计划只有公司的前10％或20％的客户才能使用。因此，他认为，企业有机会“为普通客户提供优质服务，这些客户在40年前可能还不被认为是重要客户，但如今却正在建立忠诚度行为模式。”
  他补充说，使奖励计划可供更多客户使用对许多企业来说都是额外的好处，因为“无论是大型银行还是大型旅游公司，他们都开始从这些钱包中积蓄数十亿美元。”这些点可能永远不会被赎回，但从会计角度来看，它们仍被视为负债。
 Tap Network旨在通过允许客户通过更广泛的奖励网络（通常可以在较低的积分级别上兑换）来消费这些积分来解决此问题。它是现有奖励计划的白标添加，每个计划都选择最适合其客户的奖励。
 例如，Uber最近与Tap Network合作，扩展了其Uber Rewards计划，提供了由Tap Network提供动力的新奖励，例如免费的Apple Music或HBO Max，以及向世界中央厨房等慈善机构捐款的选项。要求奖励的最低积分从500点降低到100点。
 使用Tap Network的其他公司包括华纳音乐集团（如前所述，它也是投资者）和专注于隐私的浏览器公司Brave。 
戴说，未来，Tap甚至可以允许消费者组合来自不同程序的奖励积分：“如果我想兑换某些东西，我可能能够获得Uber积分，Warner积分， 另一个程序的一点”，然后将它们结合起来。
note_en:
You may recognize the Tap Network name, as well as its co-founder and CEO  Lin Dai,   from Hooch, a startup that  offered a drink-a-day subscription service before shifting focus to  a broader rewards program. Dai told me he “learned a lot from the Hooch experience” but ultimately “decided that Tap is a much bigger opportunity, we’re really looking at rewards in general.”
 So Tap Network is a new startup, one that recently raised $4 million in funding from investors including Revelis Capital, Nima Capital, the Forbes family office, Warner Music Group, Access Industries, Bill Tai, Bob Hurst, Edward Devlin and others.
  Dai said that normal rewards programs are only accessible to the top 10% or 20% of a company’s customers. So in his view, businesses have an opportunity to “super serve the average customers who 40 years ago might not have been considered important customers, but who today could be building a loyalty behavior pattern.”
  He added that making rewards programs accessible to more customers has an added benefit for many businesses, because “whether it’s a major bank or major travel company, they are starting to accrue billions of dollars that are locked up in these wallets.” Those points might never be redeemed, but they’re still considered liabilities from an accounting perspective.
 Tap Network aims to solve this problem by allowing customers to spend those points through a broader network of rewards, which can usually be redeemed at a lower point level. It’s offered as a white-label addition to an existing rewards program, with each program choosing the rewards that might be the best fit for their customers.
 For example, Uber recently worked with Tap Network to  expand its Uber Rewards program, offering new Tap Network-powered rewards like free Apple Music or HBO Max, as well as the option to donate to causes like World Central Kitchen. And the minimum number of points needed to claim a reward fell from 500 points to 100 points.
 Other companies using Tap Network include Warner Music Group (which, as previously mentioned, is also an investor) and  privacy-focused browser company Brave.
 Dai said that in the future, Tap could even allow consumers to combine rewards points from different programs: “If I want to redeem something, I might be able to take a little bit of my Uber points, a little bit of my Warner points, a little bit of points from another program” and combine them.
title: Tap Network为其可定制的奖励计划筹集了400万美元
title_en: Tap Network raises $4M for its customizable rewards program

如果您或您认识的某个人有自杀或自残的念头，请致电1-800-273-8255，在美国任何地方拨打免费的国家自杀预防生命线，或致电741741，向危机文本热线发送HOME 。
 COVID-19，野火烟雾和飓风，机体混乱的政治局势以及世界上所有的麻烦，最近让我寻求庇护。我一直在想我的密歇根叔叔，以及他如何过大而过世。我想告诉你关于他的事。也许在他的故事中可以学到一些东西：一种不生活，一种不死的方法，以及在我们如雨后春笋般恢复原状之后如何做出更好的选择，这就是所谓的常态。大流行时代。
 当我13岁那年，在我举行礼拜仪式之际，我的理查德叔叔给了我一组杠铃，并订阅了Penthouse。在我进入青少年时代之前，他教我如何在密歇根河中钓鳟鱼，并喜欢给我有关生活方式的哲学建议。他喜欢说：“你知道，你永远不能长得好看，晒黑或赚太多钱。”
 他第二次婚姻失败时才31岁。他宣誓离开该机构并进行了输精管结扎术。他经常告诉我妈妈，我的弟弟和妹妹是我永远不会有的孩子。
 他很英俊，有着方形的脸和笔直的希腊鼻子，下巴结实。我的继父叫他理查德·基德（Richard the Kid），因为他一直都在奔波，从不显示安定的迹象。当我祖母曾经责骂他“生活比玩乐更重要”时，他会说她错了。 “妈妈，生活就是一切。”
 在他58岁的大部分时间里，他住在庞蒂亚克塞内卡山丘出生地的十英里内。他于1970年通过了密歇根州律师考试，然后进入奥克兰县检察官办公室。他走过迪克·莱文（Dick Levine），并发现了陪审团辩论的天赋。 “我很好，我判了一个无辜男子的罪名，”他告诉他的一位前妻，后者要求我不要使用她的名字。
 1973年，他换了个头，挂了一个木瓦：“理查德·杰罗姆·莱文，刑事辩护律师。”他为各种各样的人辩护，从酒后驾车的人到毒贩，再到被控强奸约会的男子。他拒绝了辩诉交易和幕后交易。他因一连串的无罪开庭而在县法院中出名，有人说这种开庭到七十年代。他以十大节日的组织者而闻名，这是他与其他九名律师一起在附近的Keego港口的宴会厅Santia Hall举办的年度假日庆典。他总是和他的好友帕特里克（Patrick）一起演唱“蓝色圣诞节”。 
然后，突然之间，在2002年的万圣节之夜，他在奥克兰县划船俱乐部为自己举办了一场派对。一张海报说：“再见，里奇。”他的朋友艾伦·桑蒂亚（Irene Santia）是桑蒂亚·霍尔（Santia Hall）的所有者，为这一盛事提供了美食。有鸡尾酒和开胃小菜，以庆祝他在科罗拉多州斯廷博特斯普林斯的新生活计划。有人问他什么时候回来。
 2003年7月21日，两名在密歇根州北部南部分支松河钓鱼的人通知阿尔科纳县警长部门，在离营地几百码的一片森林中发现了白色的雪佛兰马里布。附近有一个小的橙色圆顶帐篷，一个冷却器，灯笼和一个仍在书包中的睡袋。在河岸上，有四根弓形的雪松树遮蔽着一对茶色的水池，那里是一根钓鱼竿。在下游是一个严重分解的尸体，脸朝上躺着，睁着眼睛，双脚指向海流，他的钓鱼涉水者聚集在脚踝周围。
 标准程序要求对凶手进行调查，但我的叔叔让现场侦探轻松了起来，这名侦探是阿尔科纳县警长的名叫詹姆斯·麦奎尔（James McGuire）的中士。十二天前，理查德（Richard）向艾琳（Erene）发送了600字的电子邮件，其中他清楚地说明了一切：他已经计划了多年的去世时间和地点。离婚聚会是他的觉醒。他感谢她成为朋友。他提供了我母亲的电话号码和一个名叫里克（Rick）的童年伙伴的电话号码（他要求我不要使用他的姓氏）。
 验尸官在我叔叔的肺里没发现水。他也没有心脏病发作。他们最终发现的是毒药：致命剂量的酒精和焦虑药物。麦奎尔裁定死亡为自杀。
 当我收到这封电子邮件的消息时，我从加利福尼亚的伯克利开车往南去了我祖母在奥兰治县的家。然后，在发现尸体两周后的8月初，我再次开车向南行驶，这次是去我母亲在圣地亚哥的家中，在那里我走进了一个可怕的场景。母亲焦躁不安地冲进来，而我89岁的祖母则显得木乃伊说：“他为什么要这样做？”家人和朋友挤在客厅里。有人读了卡迪什。然后我们做了悲伤的犹太人所做的事情：我们吃了。
 几周后，艾琳（Erene）和理查德（Richard）的前邻居拉尔夫·迪特里克（Ralph Diettrick）一起驱车前往密歇根州哈里斯维尔（Harrisville）镇，收集他的世俗财产。他们访问了Richard的“快乐之地”，因为他在给Irene的电子邮件中提到了Pine River的所在地。 8月中旬，里克（Rick）从东海岸出发，在密歇根州林肯（Lincoln）的吉利斯Fun仪馆（Gillies Funeral Home）收集了骨灰，然后将它们散落在松树上。他在艾琳（Erene）拥有的饭店Gino's与艾琳（Erene）和拉尔夫（Ralph）下州会面。他们喝醉了，并跳舞到我叔叔最喜欢的乐队之一迪翁（Dion）和贝尔蒙特（Belmonts）。
 艾琳（Erene）在桑蒂亚厅（Santia Hall）举行了追悼会。大约有90位朋友，同事和少数亲戚参加了会议。一位年轻的拉比在接受我母亲和理查德（Richard）的几个朋友的采访时读到了一篇致词的悼词。拉比背诵了温德尔·贝里（Wendell Berry）的《野外和平》，而不是传统的为死者祈祷。艾琳接下来发言。她没有向他致敬，而是阅读了电子邮件。没有公开共享。送葬者被释放以漫游小点心和开放式酒吧，并比较音调低沉的主唱。 
我的祖母，母亲，两个兄弟姐妹和我当时的丧气比丧亲更加丧气，为了纪念这30分钟，我们没有人飞过全国。我的母亲说她不想让我的叔叔狮子化，她把理查德的离去当作他对家庭的最后一次他妈的。十个月之内，我们掩埋了祖母，祖母在儿子去世后陷入了严重的沮丧并拒绝进食。我的母亲于2008年因癌症去世。祖父于1974年因中风去世。
 “辛纳特拉（Sinatra）唱歌，‘我做到了，’”理查德（Richard）写信给艾琳（Irene）。 “没有人比我更能做到'我的方式'。我过着自己想过的生活，现在是时候该走了。里科。”
 我记得当时在想：对您有好处，混蛋，也让我为从未听说过的昵称感到困惑。里科？里奇？迪克？理查德他到底是谁？然后我想知道我是否还在乎。
  小说家兼社会评论家哈维·斯瓦多斯（Harvey Swados）写道：“不可避免地，我们的父母是我们幻灭的见证者。” 12岁那年，无论我多么骄傲自大，父母的混乱离婚都剥夺了我的自信，我的脆弱性暴露给了我的同学，就像对残酷无情的面容一样。在那可怕的时刻，是理查德，而不是我雄心勃勃的父亲，他说：“拜托，布拉德利，走吧。”
 我沉沉沉闷，感到异常恶毒的愤怒。他奔放而自发，尽管有时有时会感到不适，但他的个性和明智的自我约束力令人感到安慰。尽管他享乐主义很强，但他对我的经验的无私投资以及对我重要的仅仅是承认-使我陷入密歇根州的湖泊，河流和森林中-却大有帮助。最初的尝试激发了人们对户外运动的热情，这使我在内华达山脉的岩石面和马戏团的西边找到了完整的表情。
 然而，自杀有一种囚禁幸存者的方式。我的家人度过了愉快的一天，以解构我们认识的人的性格，但是理查德？我们提起他离开。
 不过，他至少在某种意义上仍与我同在：一家人收到了一个盒子，里面装有从快乐之地回收来的少量财产。飞杆断裂，而完整且新颖的棘爪拨禾轮-一种早期但仍有效的机械阻力设计。有一个凹进的铝盒，大约相当于一个Altoids锡的大小，有六个装有弹簧的窗户，里面装有各种各样的苍蝇和若虫，还有几只彩带，似乎都没有用过。我把杆子扔了垃圾，然后将剩下的存储起来，在接下来的16年中，我进行了四步移动，每次都打包和拆箱。 
我于2017年下半年再次搬家。当我看到从未使用过的卷轴和飞箱时，我正在收拾车库。那时，我离开公司去了新闻学院。我与西北，哥伦比亚和伯克利等二十多岁的人一起在一些户外杂志（包括《 Outside》）上做过编辑实习。作为一名记者，我发现与追逐相比，故事的实际写作显得苍白：寻找潜伏的真相，无论它在哪里被发现。我喜欢跟随线索进入尘土飞扬的档案馆，并追寻几十年来从未听到过的人物。追逐使我脱离了头脑，在那里我花了太多时间，而进入了世界。
 而现在，在我自己的车库里一个破烂的齿轮箱里，是一个值得解决的神秘奥秘，可能与我的家族史有关。我从来不认识我的叔叔会飞鱼，他把它和势利者联系在一起。他用超轻便的套装捕鱼，钓鳟鱼，包括短而稀的鱼竿，细小的旋转渔线轮和Mepps或Panther Martin诱饵。飞线轮看上去几乎没用过。我想他是在出西的同一年买的。
 没什么意义。如果您计划在一年内结束生命，为什么要接受飞蝇钓这样的学科？我回想起我第一次听说他移居汽船的经历。
 “你坐下吗？”我的母亲在2002年秋天的某个时候通过电话对我说。“您的叔叔正在出售一切，成为滑雪迷。”
 在15年内第五次职业变更期间，我住在加利福尼亚的伯克利山庄。
 “布拉德利？你听到我了吗？他要搬到科罗拉多州。”她说，他计划晚上照看酒吧，白天滑雪和钓鱼。
 “理查德要离开密歇根州吗？”我说。 “好吗？”那时我叫他理查德。他当时57岁，我40岁。她说他已经卖掉了自己的房子，向北的滑雪公寓，船，摩托车—除了弹出式露营者和皮卡车以外的所有东西。 
现在，将近17年后，与理查德（Richard）西移的年龄接近，我决定了解离开密歇根州的叔叔很重要。还有那位回来的叔叔。
 理查德（Richard）在偶尔访问加利福尼亚时从东到西乘火车旅行，所以我制定了在相反方向做同样的计划。在2019年5月中旬，我在加利福尼亚和风（California Zephyr）上预订了一个小卧铺，将卷轴和飞行箱装进了一个滚动袋，然后在内华达州里诺登上了火车。我计划在芝加哥下车，租车，然后开车去密歇根州探望理查德的老地方。最终，我要向北去快乐的地方。我会在回程时参观汽船。
 我锁上了房间的门，拉开了窗帘，然后开始整理笔记，其中包括麦奎尔中士的事件报告。我还采访了我叔叔的熟人，两位前妻，老恋人，律师，滑雪伙伴，可追溯到庞蒂亚克的莱文家族的朋友（包括我叔叔最好的朋友里克和一个名叫史蒂夫·怀曼的人），以及每个人的生活。愿意分享回忆的亲戚。
  这些谈话汇聚成一个惊人的启示：理查德（Richard）是伪装，模仿的大师。一位堂兄对我们所有人说：“他的死让我看到我从他那儿得到的那部分只是他真实面额的2％或10％或20％。其他部分是什么？”
 这些部分将逐渐显示出来。例如，我以为我叔叔喜欢火车旅行，因为那里的纯净电影院从草原到草原再到山脉。但是据我了解，火车也是他运动生涯的延长，因为他会打俱乐部车，使用单缸皮试着去接女性。
 出发前，我发现了另一个奇怪的发现。我登录了奥克兰郡契据数据库，那里有一个奇怪的线索：理查德（Richard）离开科罗拉多州的那一刻，他的家就被收回了。
 理查德（Richard）于1945年战后出生于庞蒂亚克（Pontiac），当时该国乃至汽车城繁荣昌盛。家庭电影显示他处于运动过度的婴儿期，挣扎着从高脚椅上跳下来，,着婴儿围栏，决心逃脱任何抱着他的东西。在他两岁的时候，他残酷地拍打着家人的大牧羊犬，三脚踩着三轮车拍了拍他的场面。是他的两倍大，在密歇根州北部的一个湖泊中，大约五到六只全尺寸划艇的船桨工作，每只船的拉力足以将他抬离座位。 
您几乎可以看到他在驱除已经掌握的其他力量。反犹太主义猖ramp-底特律电台神父查尔斯·科夫林神父从当地广播电台WJR向全国观众散播反犹太情绪，庞蒂亚克的犹太社区盘旋在车上。我的祖父母是这个世界的领导者：他是一家鞋类零售商，她与他并肩工作，他们的大部分时间都花在了一个小的改革会众上。我的祖父是塔斯卡卢萨人（Tuscaloosa）的本地人，他从阿拉巴马大学的法学院辍学谋生，他在追求知识方面是拉比裔。他的儿子不是。
 即使在小时候，理查德还是祖父神圣的亵渎。我的祖母是个高大，富豪，和cious的女人，使家里安宁。我的母亲比理查德（Richard）大六岁，她本人很坚强，但是11岁那年就去上大学。
 同年，我祖父遭受了第一次心脏病发作和中风，最终导致他丧生。在理查德（Richard）的最后一封信中，他将父亲最初的心脏病发作称为“音乐去世的那一天”。
 从那天起，我们家的生活永远是残酷的-一直担心我父亲的健康，一直担心钱。那时我向自己发誓，当生活变得对我不利时，我不会呆在身边。
 当年他13岁时，他正在剃须刀并被女孩子迷住了，他试图说服里克和史蒂夫跳下车库的屋顶。他曾在庞蒂亚克中心（Pontiac Central）成为过时的斗士，踢过足球和网球，在学校玩过戏，而且很有魅力，可以在伍德沃德大街（Woodward Avenue）著名的免税汉堡店Ted's接女孩。里克将他形容为种马，叛军和坏男孩。
 他参加了俄亥俄州的迈阿密并加入了一个聚会。 1967年夏天，在大学和法学院之间，他搬到了旧金山，住在海特，找到了移动家具的工作。他曾经告诉我说他不参加“夏日之恋”，因为他发现大多数嬉皮士都是郊区的犹太孩子们的特权。 1968年6月，即他进入法学院的那年，他嫁给了一个在犹太教堂的混音台认识的高中女友。两年后，她申请离婚。他于同年通过律师资格，成为助理检察官。他得到了选秀号码，但由于皮肤长期病故而被免于越南入境。他在72年从法院娶了一名社会工作者，并在73年成为辩护律师。三年后，他的妻子因为外遇而离开了他。
 他没有感情，对感性过敏。但是大多数人发现他的金钱和时间都很友善。他自愿参加了特奥会和人道协会。他在社区大学任教。他主持了夏季烧烤：到处都是牛排和饮料。女人，尤其是刚脱离恋爱关系的女人，发现他很有魅力，热情而有趣。男人和孩子们喜欢他，因为他很幽默和好玩。他可以听别人的忧虑而不必自嘲。他发誓要爱家人，尤其是我的母亲。当他好时，他非常好。 
但是他对性的渴望似乎从未减弱。史蒂夫告诉我：“他想成为一个无法抗拒的女人。” “他想得到由此产生的性爱，但即使没有，他仍然想成为那个女人会想到的人：我想和那个男人上床。”或者，正如他在去世前不久告诉某人的那样，“我是母亲最糟糕的噩梦。”
 还有更多。当他三十多岁时，他告诉法务秘书，他在夏令营中遭到一名男子的性虐待。 “我没有意识到这是骚扰，”他告诉她。 “对我来说感觉很好。”
 同时，作为辩护律师，他为那些被指控强奸约会的男人辩护得如此出色，以至于有人质疑他的道德，包括我。他曾经告诉史蒂夫，关键是陪审团的选择。他说：“我希望陪审团中有女性，有工作的女性。” “我要收银员。我想要低端的售货员。因为她们比其他男人更难对待其他女性。他们的态度是，亲爱的，如果您不想要它，晚上11点在他房间里到底在干什么？”他的律师和女性化似乎是同一个看起来不道德的，有时令人反感的道德体系的一部分，而这就是我们分开的地方。
 他的前妻和家人证实，他的父母给他施加了巨大的压力，他们俩都很清楚地意识到了别人的意见，这使他背负了沉重的负担。他是一个模棱两可的犹太人，敏锐地意识到自己的宗教信仰，每年都参加我母亲每年举行的逾越节晚餐，但是他从意第绪语中回避说我祖母经常与他人结缘：月经。至少在我家庭的宇宙学中，可以考虑做一个正确的事情，成为一个完美的父亲，养家糊口的人和社区成员。他以为是个虚假的人，但我发现他从不相信自己会成为现实。
 至于他的自杀电子邮件：充满了虚张声势和陈词滥调，但由于这是他留下的唯一线索，因此被家人和朋友解构，许多解释都相互矛盾。有人将纸条看作是鲱鱼，他的死亡减去他做出的凯旋飞跃，而不是摆脱生活的沉闷洗牌。其他人认为他患有身体或精神疾病，或两者兼有，尽管他几乎没有显示出任何一个问题的迹象。另一个营地注意到了面子，因为理查德偶尔宣布自己不在乎变老，并且曾经告诉一个朋友，没有比死鳟鱼更好的死法了。
 我不知道该怎么想他的死似乎与某种先天的自私和固执相符，但闻起来更像是因绝望而产生的悲剧。
 他是否曾因担任刑事辩护律师而遭受过替代性创伤？他是一个自恋者，无法应对自己的褪色容貌，膝盖不好，金钱损失吗？太虚荣或沮丧，无法重新开始？他告诉一些朋友，他对性失去了兴趣，这可能是药物，抑郁症或年龄引起的副作用。从我母亲那里，我们知道他正在服用药物来预防惊恐发作，他在某种程度上因为担心自己会心脏病发作而在法庭上崩溃而遭受痛苦。他遭受了灾难性的财务挫折，如果受到重挫，将使他的个性和生活方式丧失。正如菲利普·罗斯（Philip Roth）曾经写道的一个举止高尚的中年男性角色：“没有任何存在可以说明其任性构成其唯一权威并为其提供主要娱乐的存在。” 
“真他妈的浪费，”他的一位前妻告诉我，总结了自己对自己的所作所为。 “真他妈的浪费。”  火车绕过科罗拉多河，绕过格伦伍德峡谷，然后穿过戈尔  ......

If you or someone you know is having thoughts of suicide or self-harm, call the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline toll-free from anywhere in the United States at 1-800-273-8255, or text HOME to the Crisis Text Line at 741741.
 COVID-19, wildfire smoke and hurricanes, the chaotic state of the body politic, and all the troubles in the world have me seeking shelter lately. I’ve been thinking about my Michigan uncle, and how he lived large and died too young. I’d like to tell you about him. Maybe there’s something to be learned in his story: a way not to live, and a way not to die, and how to make better choices after we’re sprung loose to resume whatever it is we call normalcy in the dawning of the post-pandemic era.
 When I turned 13, on the occasion of my bar mitzvah, my Uncle Richard gave me a set of barbells and a subscription to  Penthouse. He taught me how to fish for trout in Michigan rivers before I entered my teens and liked to give me philosophical tips on ways to live. “You know,” he was fond of saying, “you can never be too good-looking, too tanned, or make too much money.”
 He was 31 when his second marriage failed; he swore off the institution and got a vasectomy. He would often tell my mother that my younger brother and sister and I were the children he’d never have.
 He was handsome, with a square face and a straight Greek nose and a strong chin. My stepfather called him Richard the Kid, because he was always on the make and never showed signs of settling down. When my grandmother used to chide him that “there’s more to life than having fun,” he’d say she was wrong. “ All there is to life is having fun, Mom.”
 For most of his 58 years, he lived within ten miles of his birthplace in Pontiac’s Seneca Hills. He passed the Michigan bar exam in 1970 and then entered the Oakland County Prosecutor’s Office. He went by Dick Levine, and he discovered a gift for arguing jury trials. “I’m so good, I convicted an innocent man,” he told one of his ex-wives, who asked me not to use her name.
 In 1973, he switched sides and hung a shingle: “Richard Jerome Levine, Criminal Defense Attorney.” He defended a wide range of people, from drunk drivers to drug dealers to men charged with date rape. He spurned plea bargains and backroom deals. He became famous in the county court for a streak of acquittals that some say reached into the seventies. He was renowned as the organizer of the Big Ten party, an annual holiday bash he threw with nine other attorneys at Santia Hall, a banquet space in nearby Keego Harbor. He always sang “Blue Christmas” with his buddy Patrick.
 And then, suddenly, on Halloween night in 2002, he threw a party for himself at the Oakland County Boat Club. A poster said “BYE BYE RICHIE.” His friend Irene Santia, the owner of Santia Hall, catered the event. There were cocktails and hors d’oeuvres, a celebration of his plans for a new life in Steamboat Springs, Colorado. Someone asked him when he would come back.
 On July 21, 2003, two men who’d been fishing northern Michigan’s South Branch  Pine River alerted the sheriff’s department in Alcona County that a white Chevy Malibu had been found in the clearing of a forest a few hundred yards away from a campground. Nearby was a small orange dome tent, a cooler, lanterns, and a sleeping bag still in its stuffsack. On the bank of the river, where four bowed cedar trees shaded a pair of tea-colored pools, was a fishing rod. Just downstream was a badly decomposed body, lying face up, eyes open, feet pointed into the current, his fishing waders gathered around the ankles.
 Standard procedure dictated a homicide investigation, but my uncle had made it easy for the detective at the scene, an Alcona County sheriff’s sergeant by the name of James McGuire. Twelve days earlier, Richard had sent a 600-word email to Irene, in which he spelled everything out: He had planned the time and place of his death for years. The going-away party had been his wake. He thanked her for being a friend. He provided both my mother’s phone number and that of a childhood buddy named Rick (who asked me not to use his last name).
 The coroner found no water in my uncle’s lungs. Nor had he suffered a heart attack. What they did find, eventually, was poison: a lethal dose of alcohol and anxiety medications. McGuire ruled the death a suicide.
 When I got the news of the email, I drove south from Berkeley, California,  to my grandmother’s home in Orange County. And then, in early August, two weeks  after the body was found, I drove south again, this time to my mother’s house in San Diego, where I walked into a terrible scene. My mother fretted and stormed, while my 89-year-old grandmother looked mummified, saying, “Why would he do such a thing?” Family and friends huddled in the living room. Someone read the kaddish. And then we did what grieving Jews do: we ate.
 Several weeks later, Irene, along with a former neighbor of Richard’s, Ralph Diettrick, drove to the town of Harrisville, Michigan, to gather what remained of his worldly possessions. They visited Richard’s “Happy Place,” as he’d referred to the Pine River location in his email to Irene. In mid-August, Rick traveled from the East Coast to collect the cremains at Gillies Funeral Home in Lincoln, Michigan, later scattering them at the Pine. He met with Irene and Ralph downstate at Gino’s, a restaurant Irene owned. They got drunk and danced to Dion and the Belmonts, one of my uncle’s favorite bands.
 Irene hosted the memorial at Santia Hall. Ninety or so friends, colleagues, and a handful of relatives attended. A young rabbi read a eulogy cobbled together from interviews with my mother and a few of Richard’s friends. The rabbi recited Wendell Berry’s  “The Peace of Wild Things” rather than the traditional prayers for the dead. Irene spoke next. Rather than eulogize him, she read the email. There was no public sharing. The mourners were released to roam the canapés and open bar and to compare notes sotto voce.
 My grandmother, mother, two siblings, and I were more aggrieved than bereaved at that point, and none of us flew across the country for this 30-minute remembrance. My mother said she wouldn’t have wanted to lionize my uncle, and she referred to Richard’s departure as his final fuck you to the family. Within ten months, we buried my grandmother, who plunged into a profound depression after her son’s death and refused to eat. My mother died of cancer in 2008. My grandfather had died of a stroke in 1974.
 “Sinatra sang, ‘I did it my way,’” Richard had written to Irene. “Nobody ever did it more ‘my way’ than me. I lived the life I wanted to live, and now it’s time for me to go. Rico.”
 I remember thinking, Good for you, asshole, and also puzzling over the nickname I’d never heard. Rico? Richie? Dick? Richard? Who the hell was he? And I wondered then whether I even cared.
  “Inevitably our parents are the bearers of our disillusion,” wrote the novelist and social critic Harvey Swados. At age 12, whatever cocky self-confidence I had was stripped clean by the chaotic divorce of my parents, and my vulnerability was exposed, as plain as a facial blemish to my schoolmates, whose cruelty was merciless. In that horrible time, it was Richard, and not my ambitious father, who lifted me from my dark moods by saying, “C’mon, Bradley, let’s go.”
 I was brooding and sullen and given to unusually vicious fits of rage. He was jocose and spontaneous, and his force of personality and wisecracking self-assurance were comforting albeit sometimes jarring. Despite his many hedonisms, his selfless investment in my experience and the mere acknowledgment that I mattered—plunking me down in Michigan lakes, rivers, and forests—helped a great deal. Those first forays seeded a passion for the outdoors that would find full expression for me out west on the rock faces and cirques of the Sierra Nevada.
 And yet, suicides have a way of imprisoning the survivors. My family enjoyed spending days deconstructing the personalities of the people we had known, but Richard? We filed him away.
 He was still with me in at least one sense, though: the family received a box containing the few possessions recovered from the Happy Place. There was a broken fly rod and an intact and newish click-pawl reel—an early and still effective mechanical drag design. There was a dented aluminum box about the size of an Altoids tin, with six spring-loaded windows that held an assortment of dry flies and nymphs and a few streamers, none of which appeared to have been used. I trashed the rod and stored the rest, which I packed and unpacked in each of the four moves I made over the next 16 years.
 I moved again in late 2017. I was packing up the garage when I saw the reel and fly box, which I’d never used. At that point, I’d left business and gone to journalism school. I’d done editorial internships alongside twentysomethings from Northwestern and Columbia and Berkeley at a string of outdoor magazines, including  Outside. As a journalist, I’d found that the actual writing of a story pales compared to the chase: the search for the lurking truth, wherever it might be found. I like to follow leads into dusty archives and track down characters who haven’t been heard from in decades. The chase gets me out of my head, where I spend too much time as it is, and into the world.
 And now, here in a battered gear bin in my own garage, was a worthy mystery begging to be solved, one that might have bearing on my family history. I’d never known my uncle to fly-fish—he associated it with snobs. He fished for trout with an ultralight outfit, which consisted of a short, whippy rod, a small spinning reel, and a Mepps or a Panther Martin lure. The fly reel looked barely used. I figured he’d bought it the same year he went out west.
 Which didn’t make sense. Why take up a discipline like fly-fishing if you planned to end your life within the year? I thought back to when I first heard about his move to Steamboat.
 “Are you sitting down?” my mother had said to me by phone, sometime in the autumn of 2002. “Your uncle is selling everything to become a ski bum.”
 I was living in California’s Berkeley Hills, in the midst of a fifth career change in 15 years.
 “Bradley? Do you hear me? He’s moving to Colorado.” She said he planned to tend bar at night, to ski and fish during the day.
 “Richard’s leaving Michigan?” I said. “For good?” By then I called him Richard. He was 57, and I was 40. She said he’d already sold his house, a ski condo up north, boats, a motorcycle—everything but a pop-up camper and a pickup.
 Now, nearly 17 years later, approaching the same age as Richard when he moved west, I decided it was important to understand the uncle who left Michigan. And the uncle who returned.
 Richard traveled by rail from east to west on his occasional visits to California, so I made plans to do the same in the opposite direction. In mid-May of 2019, I booked a tiny sleeper on the California Zephyr, packed the reel and fly box into a rolling bag, and boarded the train in Reno, Nevada. I planned to get off in Chicago, rent a car, and drive to Michigan to visit Richard’s old haunts. Eventually, I’d go north, to the Happy Place. I’d visit Steamboat on the return leg.
 I latched the door to the room, drew the curtains, and began to assemble notes, which included Sergeant McGuire’s incident report. I had also interviewed my uncle’s acquaintances, two ex-wives, old lovers, fellow attorneys, ski buddies, friends of the Levine family dating back to Pontiac (including my uncle’s best friends, Rick and a man named Steve Wyman), and every living relative who was willing to share memories.
  Those conversations converged on a stunning revelation: Richard had been a master of disguise, of dissimulation. A cousin spoke for us all when she said, “His death made me see that the piece I got from him was just maybe 2 or 10 or 20 percent of who he really was. What were the other parts?”
 Those parts would reveal themselves gradually. I assumed, for instance, that my uncle loved train travel for the pure cinema of the landscape transforming from prairie to steppe to mountains. But as I learned, the train was also an extension of his sporting life, because he would work the club car, using one-liners to try and pick up women.
 Before my departure, I made another odd discovery. I logged on to the Oakland County Register of Deeds database, and there, for anyone curious enough to have looked, was a clue: Richard’s home had been repossessed right around the time he left for Colorado.
 Richard was born in Pontiac in postwar 1945, on the cusp of prosperous times for the country—and the Motor City itself. Home movies show him in hyperkinetic infancy, struggling to jump from a high chair, mauling bars of a playpen, determined to escape anything holding him in. There are scenes of him at age two brazenly slapping the family’s immense collie, at three pedaling a trike twice his size, and at about five or six working the oars of a full-size rowboat in one of northern Michigan’s lakes, each pull forceful enough to raise him off his seat.
 You could almost see him exorcising some other force that had already taken hold. Anti-Semitism was rampant—Detroit’s Father Charles Coughlin, the Radio Priest, spewed anti-Jewish sentiment to a nationwide audience from a local radio station, WJR—and Pontiac’s Jewish community circled its wagons. My grandparents were leaders in that world: he was a shoe retailer, she worked alongside him, and they devoted much of their lives to a small reform congregation. My grandfather, a native of Tuscaloosa who had dropped out of the University of Alabama’s law school to earn a living, was rabbinic in his pursuit of knowledge. His son was not.
 Even as a child, Richard was the profane to my grandfather’s sacred. My grandmother, a tall, regal, gracious woman, made peace in the household. My mother, who was six years older than Richard, was strong-willed herself but went off to college when he was only 11.
 That was the same year my grandfather suffered the first of the heart attacks and strokes that would eventually kill him. In Richard’s last note, he referred to his father’s initial heart attack as “the Day the Music Died.”
 From that day on life in our home was forever grim—constant worries about my dad’s health, constant worries about money. I vowed to myself then that when life became grim for me, I wouldn’t stay around.
 He was shaving and besotted by girls  at 13, the year he was bar mitzvahed, and he tried to talk Rick and Steve into jumping off the garage roof. He became a sometime brawler at Pontiac Central, played football and tennis, acted in school plays, and was charismatic enough to pick up girls at Ted’s, Woodward Avenue’s famous drive-in burger joint. Rick described him variously as a stallion, a rebel, and a bad boy.
 He attended Miami of Ohio and joined a frat. In the summer of 1967, between college and law school, he moved to San Francisco, lived in the Haight, and found work moving furniture. He once told me that he didn’t partake in the Summer of Love because he found most of the hippies to be privileged Jewish kids from the suburbs. In June of 1968, the same year he entered law school, he married a high school girlfriend he’d met at a synagogue mixer. She filed for divorce two years later. He passed the bar the same year and became an assistant prosecutor. He got a draft number but was exempted from Vietnam because of a chronic skin condition. He married a social worker from the courts in ’72 and became a defense attorney in ’73. Three years later, his wife left him because he was having an affair.
 He didn’t do feelings and was allergic to sentimentality. But most people found him kind and giving, both of his money and his time. He volunteered for the Special Olympics and the Humane Society. He taught at a community college. He hosted summer barbecues: steaks and drinks all around. Women, especially those newly out of relationships, found him charismatic, intense, and fun. Men and children liked him because he was jolly and playful. He could listen to someone’s woes without becoming self-referential. He avowed love of his family, especially my mother. When he was good, he was very good.
 But he was driven by a hunger for sex that never seemed to abate. “What he wanted to become was someone irresistible to women,” Steve told me. “He wanted to get the sex that resulted from it, but even if it didn’t, he still wanted to be that person that women would think: I would like to go to bed with that guy.” Or, as he told someone not long before his death, “I’m a mother’s worst nightmare.”
 There was more. When he was in his thirties, he told a legal secretary that he’d been sexually abused by a man at summer camp. “I didn’t realize it was molestation,” he told her. “It felt pretty good to me.”
 Meanwhile, as a defense attorney, he defended men who’d been accused of date rape so deviously well that some questioned his morality, including me. He once told Steve that the key was jury selection. “I want women on the jury—working women,” he said. “I want cashiers. I want low-end salesclerks. Because they are harder on other women than any man could ever be. Their attitude is, Honey, if you weren’t looking for it, what the hell were you doing up in his room at 11 o’clock at night?” His lawyering and womanizing seemed to be part of the same unseemly, sometimes repugnant ethical system, and this is where we parted ways.
 His ex-wives and family members confirmed that he was burdened by incredible pressure from his parents, both of whom were exquisitely conscious of the opinions of others. He was an ambivalent Jew, acutely aware of the religion to which he’d been born, dutifully attending the Passover seder my mother held every year, but he shied from a Yiddish term that my grandmother often attached to others: mensch. A mensch, at least in my family’s cosmology, could be counted on to do the right thing, to be the perfect father, breadwinner, community member. A mensch he assumed he was not, and I gathered that he never believed he could be.
 As for his suicide email: it was full of bravado and clichés, but because it was the only clue he left behind, it was deconstructed by family and friends, with many interpretations advanced and contradicted. Some viewed the note as a red herring, his death less the triumphal leap he made it out to be than a piteous shuffle away from life. Others assumed he was either physically or mentally ill, or both, although he’d shown little sign of either problem. Another camp took the note at face value, because Richard had proclaimed occasionally that he didn’t care to grow old and had once told a friend that there would be no better way to die than in a trout stream.
 I didn’t know what to think. His death seemed consistent with a certain inborn selfishness and stubbornness, but it smelled more like a tragic act born of despair.
 Had he suffered vicarious trauma from a career as a criminal defense attorney? Was he a narcissist who couldn’t deal with his fading looks, bad knees, loss of money—too vain or depressed to begin again? He’d told some friends he’d lost his interest in sex, a possible side effect of medication, depression, or age. From my mother, we know he was on medication to stave off panic attacks, which he suffered in part from worrying that he would have a heart attack and collapse in court. He had suffered a cataclysmic financial setback, which, if exposed, would strip him of persona and lifestyle. As Philip Roth once wrote of a libidinous middle-aged male character: “There was no unsnarling an existence whose waywardness constituted its only authority and provided its primary amusement.”
 “What a fucking waste,” one of his ex-wives told me, summing up what he’d done to himself. “What a fucking waste.”

在等待有关在美国使用第二种疫苗的授权的消息时，出现小故障的现象一直在通过联邦政府的“经纱速度”进行分配。 ;本周，美国首次看到了由辉瑞公司与BioNTech合作开发的疫苗。但是紧随其后，许多州开始表示随后几周的装运订单已被削减。联邦政府中的一些人指出问题可能出在生产后，辉瑞公司发表了一份声明，指出其仓库中已准备好将药物运出，但没有指示将其运往何处。总而言之，这是您在进行此类大型工作的最初几周内的期望。
  报告问题的第一个州是伊利诺伊州，其州长普里兹克（J.B. Pritzker）表示，有迹象表明下周将只接受辉瑞/ BioNTech疫苗预期剂量的一半。此后，已有十几个州表示他们将在第二周收到比计划少的剂量（这篇文章似乎有相当全面的清单）。
 除了明显减少了疫苗接种人数的问题外，这还给那些正在使用基于风险的优先级为一组人群接种疫苗的州带来了问题。例如，医护人员的剂量可用性将决定何时可以将疫苗转移到养老院人群；计划何时更改目标人群对于快速有效地转移至关重要。不知道何时会有足够的疫苗，要管理这些过渡就困难得多。
    关于裁员的原因，我们听到了许多借口。在关于分发工作的新闻发布会上，卫生与公共服务部长将矛头指向了辉瑞公司，该公司正在美国生产这种疫苗。阿扎尔说：“最近，他们最近被告知他们在制造过程中可能面临的各种挑战，我们将确保通过任何机制，为他们提供全力支持，以确保他们能够为美国人民生产。” 。
 但是，阿扎尔不是政府唯一讲的故事。华盛顿邮报被告知，该问题与确定一周的剂量有关。原本计划在星期五进行，这是整周的制造工作完成的时间；从那以后，它已转移到星期二，届时将减少剂量。这必然会减少提供给各州的可用性数字。
 不幸的是，这些似乎都不是完全正确的。针对这一消息，辉瑞公司发表了一份声明，称该公司没有生产问题。 ＆＃34;本周，我们成功地将美国政府要求我们运送的290万剂全部运送到他们指定的地点，＆＃34;声明如下。 ＆＃34;我们仓库中还有数百万剂药，但是截至目前，我们还没有收到任何有关其他剂药的装运说明。
 鉴于BioNTech之所以加入辉瑞，部分原因是后者具有制造能力，并且由于特朗普政府在COVID-19主题方面存在明显的信誉问题，因此辉瑞很可能会与辉瑞合作陈述更接近事实。 
Warp Speed是一个庞大而复杂的项目，多个疫苗生产商最终将把剂量运送给联邦政府，然后由联邦政府将其分发给各州，每个州都必须计划根据不同的优先级将其分发给人群。会有一些成长的烦恼一点也不奇怪。希望可以在只有一家疫苗供应商的情况下解决这些问题，这在一定程度上限制了复杂性。
    但是，根据FDA咨询委员会关于Moderna疫苗有效的决定，单一供应商的状况最早可能会在今天改变。根据初步数据，Moderna预计，一旦获得FDA的正式紧急使用授权，它将立即提供更多的疫苗，这将使分销复杂化但缓解供应瓶颈。
 从长远来看，有迹象表明还有其他机会增加疫苗供应。在今年早些时候辉瑞公司拒绝提供额外剂量的疫苗的提议之后，联邦政府可能接近达成购买更多疫苗的协议。辉瑞公司已经与其原料供应商合作，以确定可以生产多少疫苗。当美国拒绝该药时，该公司只是找到其他国家向其出售药物。现在，这表明如果美国政府可以确保增加其疫苗成分的供应，它将向该国出售更多剂量。
 截至目前，美国仅承诺购买足够的疫苗，以在2021年中期之前覆盖一半的人口，因此必须增加剂量。上周，政府确实获得了Moderna的承诺，承诺再提供1亿剂疫苗。主要药房连锁店预计今年春天将有广泛的供应，这表明有信心可以选择更多的供应。
 对于目前的供应限制而言，本周显而易见的一个小帮助是正常的过量填充。在疫苗瓶中发现的这种药有时足以允许从五剂瓶的辉瑞疫苗中提取额外的剂量。尽管它无法解决供应大局的问题，但无疑将帮助各个机构在疫苗接种计划的初期保护更多员工。
note_en:
As we're waiting for word on the authorization of  a second vaccine for use in the US, glitches have been striking the distribution of the first through the federal government's "Operation Warp Speed." This week, the US saw the first use of the vaccine developed by a Pfizer/BioNTech collaboration. But immediately afterward, many states started saying that orders for shipments in the ensuing weeks were being cut. After some in the federal government had indicated that the problem might be in production, Pfizer issued a statement indicating that it had doses in its warehouse ready to ship out but no indication of where to ship them to. All in all, about what you'd expect in the first weeks of a massive undertaking like this.
  One of the first states to report problems was Illinois, where its governor, J.B. Pritzker, said that it had indications it would  only be receiving half the expected doses of the Pfizer/BioNTech vaccine next week. Since then, over a dozen states have indicated that they'll be receiving fewer doses than planned in the second week ( this article seems to have a fairly comprehensive list).
 Aside from the obvious problem of fewer vaccinated people, this creates issues for states that are working through vaccinating a set of populations using risk-based priorities. For example, the availability of doses for healthcare workers will determine when vaccinations can be shifted to nursing home populations; planning on when to change the target population will be critical for a quick and effective shift. Without knowing when sufficient vaccine will be available, it's much harder to manage these transitions.
    When it comes to why the orders are being cut, we're hearing multiple excuses. At a press conference on the distribution effort, Secretary of Health and Human Services  pointed the finger at Pfizer, which is manufacturing the vaccine in the US. “We have recently been informed by them, finally, of various challenges that they might have in their manufacturing and we will ensure that by whatever mechanism, that we provide them full support to ensure that they can produce for the American people,” Azar said.
 But Azar's isn't the only story being given out by the government. The Washington Post  was told that the issue was a matter of when the week's doses were determined. Originally, that had been planned for Friday, when the full week's manufacturing run had completed; it has since been shifted to Tuesday when fewer doses would be completed. That would necessarily cut down on the availability numbers given to states.
 Unfortunately, neither of these seem to be entirely true. In response to this news, Pfizer has  issued a statement in which it says it is having no production issues. "This week, we successfully shipped all 2.9 million doses that we were asked to ship by the U.S. Government to the locations specified by them," the statement reads. "We have millions more doses sitting in our warehouse but, as of now, we have not received any shipment instructions for additional doses."
 Given that BioNTech joined up with Pfizer in part because of the latter's manufacturing abilities, and due to clear credibility issues with the Trump administration on the topic of COVID-19, it's likely that Pfizer's statement is closer to the truth.
  Warp Speed is a large and complex project, in which multiple vaccine manufacturers will eventually be shipping doses to the federal government, which will then distribute them to states, each of which has to plan to get them to populations according to different priorities. It's not at all surprising that there would be some growing pains. Hopefully, they can be ironed out while there's a single vaccine supplier, which limits the complexity somewhat.
    But the single supplier status may change as early as today, based on the decision of an FDA advisory board that a vaccine from Moderna is effective. Based on preliminary figures, Moderna expects that it will supply substantially more vaccine as soon as it receives a formal Emergency Use Authorization from the FDA, which will complicate distribution but ease supply bottlenecks.
 Longer term, there are indications of other opportunities to increase vaccine supplies. After having  declined an offer from Pfizer to purchase additional doses of the vaccine earlier this year, the federal government may be  close to a deal to purchase more. Pfizer had worked with the suppliers of its raw materials to determine how much vaccine it could produce; when the US turned it down, the company simply found other countries to sell doses to. Now, it's indicating that if the US government can ensure enhanced supply of its vaccine ingredients, it will sell the additional doses to the country.
 As of right now, the US only has commitments to buy enough vaccine to cover half of its population by the middle of 2021, making additional doses essential. Last week, the government did manage to  get a commitment from Moderna for another 100 million doses of its vaccine. Major pharmacy chains are  expecting widespread availability this spring, indicating a confidence that additional supplies will be sorted out.
 One small help for the present supply constraints that's become apparent this week is that the normal "overfill" found in vaccine bottles is sometimes enough to  allow an extra dose to be extracted from a five-dose bottle of Pfizer vaccine. While it won't handle the big picture supply issues, it will undoubtedly help individual facilities get more of their staff protected in the early days of the vaccination program.
title: 疫苗已经准备好了，但是没有分发？ 操作经速遇到减速带
title_en: Vaccines ready, but no distribution? Operation Warp Speed meets speed bumps

这家印度初创公司表示，Tiger Global，Kora，卢克索，富达（FMR），D1 Capital，Baillie Gifford，Mirae和Steadview参与了这一轮融资，这使Zomato的融资后估值达到了39亿美元。 Zomato此前曾披露过约2.12亿美元的融资，作为J轮融资的一部分，这些融资来自Ant Financial，Tiger Global，Baillie Gifford和Temasek。
 Zomato的联合创始人兼首席执行官Deepinder Goyal表示，这家拥有12年历史的初创公司也正在完成一笔1.4亿美元的二次交易。他在推特上写道：“作为这项交易的一部分，我们已经向前员工提供了价值3000万美元的流动资金。”
  该初创公司最初预计到今年1月完成一轮约6亿美元的融资，但包括全球大流行在内的若干障碍推迟了筹款工作。 Zomato的投资者Info Edge在今年早些时候透露，最初承诺投资1.5亿美元的蚂蚁金服仅交付了三分之一。
 总部位于古尔冈的初创公司于今年初收购了Uber在印度的送餐业务，与Prosus Ventures支持的印度Swiggy竞争。尽管目前仅在班加罗尔的部分地区提供送餐服务，但亚马逊也出现在市场中。
 他们在一份给客户的报告中写道，印度的外卖市场正处于危险之中。伯恩斯坦的分析师预计，到2022年印度的外卖市场的价值将激增至120亿美元。 Bernstein分析师写道，Zomato拥有约50％的市场份额，是三者中目前的领导者。
 Zomato今年已经裁撤了数百个工作岗位，以改善其财务状况和应对冠状病毒大流行，这在最初几个月严重损害了印度的食品配送业务。戈亚尔说，食品配送市场“迅速摆脱了COVID-19的阴影。预计2020年12月将是我们历史上有史以来最高的GMV月。现在，我们的总交易量比2020年2月的先前峰值高出25％。”他补充说：“我对即将发生的一切以及我们将为我们的客户，配送合作伙伴和餐厅合作伙伴带来的影响感到非常兴奋。”
 9月，Goyal告诉员工，Zomato正在为其“明年下半年的某个时间”进行IPO，并正在筹集资金以开发“未来并购”的战争胸，并与任何恶意或价格战作斗争。我们在业务各个领域的竞争。” 
在印度，靠外卖赚钱尤其困难。 根据总部位于班加罗尔的研究公司RedSeer的估计，与美国等西方市场不同，在印度，每个交付商品的价值约为33美元，而类似商品的价格为4美元。  “问题在于，印度几乎没有人每天都能负担得起从食品配送公司下订单的费用，” India Quotient的风险投资家Anand Lunia在今年初接受TechCrunch采访时表示。
note_en:
The Indian startup said Tiger Global, Kora, Luxor, Fidelity (FMR), D1 Capital, Baillie Gifford, Mirae, and Steadview participated in the round, which gives Zomato a post-money valuation of $3.9 billion. Zomato had previously disclosed fundraise of about $212 million as part of Series J round from Ant Financial, Tiger Global, Baillie Gifford, and Temasek.
 Deepinder Goyal, the co-founder and chief executive of Zomato, said the 12-year-old startup is also in the process of closing a $140 million secondary transaction. “As part of this transaction, we have already provided liquidity worth $30m to our ex-employees,” he tweeted.
  The startup originally anticipated to close a round of about $600 million by January this year, but several obstacles including the global pandemic  delayed the fundraise effort. Ant Financial, which had originally committed to invest $150 million only delivered a third of it, Zomato’s investor Info Edge disclosed earlier this year.
 The Gurgaon-headquartered startup, which acquired the Indian food delivery business of Uber early this year, competes with Prosus Ventures-backed  Swiggy  in India. A third player, Amazon, has also  emerged in the market, though it currently offers its food delivery service in only parts of Bangalore.
 At stake is India’s food delivery market, which analysts at Bernstein expect to balloon to be worth $12 billion by 2022, they wrote in a report to clients — accessed by TechCrunch. With about 50% of the market share, Zomato is the current leader among the three, Bernstein analysts wrote.
 Zomato has eliminated hundreds of jobs this year to improve its finances and navigate the coronavirus pandemic, which significantly hurt the food delivery business in India in the early months. Goyal said the food delivery market is “rapidly coming out of COVID-19 shadows. December 2020 is expected to be the highest ever GMV month in our history. We are now clocking ~25% higher GMV than our previous peaks in February 2020.” He added, “I am supremely excited about what lies ahead and the impact that we will create for our customers, delivery partners, and restaurant partners.”
 In September, Goyal told employees that Zomato was working for its IPO for “sometime in the first half of next year” and was raising money to develop a war-chest for “future M&A, and fighting off any mischief or price wars from our competition in various areas of our business.”
 Making money with food delivery has been especially challenging in India. Unlike Western markets such as the U.S., where the value of each delivery item is about $33, in India, a similar item carries the price tag of $4, according to estimates by Bangalore-based research firm RedSeer.
 “The problem is that there are very few people in India who can afford to place an order from a food delivery firm each day,” said Anand Lunia, a venture capitalist at  India Quotient, in an interview with TechCrunch earlier this year.
title: 印度食品配送巨头Zomato获6.6亿美元融资

As the year dawned, venture investments into fintech startups in Latin America had grown from $50 million in 2014 to top $2.1 billion in 2020 across 139 deals,  according to a report from CB Insights.
  Investors in the round include new investors like LGT Lightstone, Tarsadia Capital, Welington Management,  e.ventures,   and an affiliate of  Advent International,   Sunley House Capital. Previous investors including SoftBank Vision Fund 1,  SoftBank   Latin America DFund, VEF, Kaszek, Vemtires and Amadeus Capital Partners also returned to put more money into the company.
 “Creditas is still in the early innings of penetrating the huge untapped secured lending market in Brazil and Mexico” says Paulo Passoni, Managing Partner of Softbank Latam fund, in a statement.
 The company’s growth is a testament both to the need for new lending products across Latin America and the perspicacity of investors like  Kaszek Ventures,   whose portfolio has included several massive wins from bets on startups tackling financial services in Latin America.
 “The journey since our investment in the Series A has been absolutely extraordinary. The team has executed on its vision, and Creditas has evolved into an asset-light ecosystem that resolves key financial needs of its customers throughout their lifetimes”, says Nicolas Szekasy, Managing Partner of Kaszek Ventures, in a statement.
 Another big winner is Redpoint’s e.ventures fund, which has focused on investments in Latin America for the last several years.
 “By empowering Brazilians to take control of their lending needs at reasonable rates, Creditas creates a beloved consumer product that will drive significant value for customers and investors. Having been involved since the seed stage through Redpoint e.ventures, we’re thrilled to support the company with our Global Growth Fund as well, as they change the Brazilian fintech landscape,” said Mathias Schilling, co-founder and Managing Partner of e.ventures.
 Creditas has plans to use the cash to expand its home and auto lending as well as a payday lending service based on customers’ salaries and a retail option to sell through buy now, pay later loans based on a customer’s salary.
 The company is also looking to expand to other markets, with an eye toward establishing a foothold in the Mexican market as well.
 Founded in 2012, when the founders worked out of a 5 squre meter office on Berrini Avenue in Sao Paulo, the company now boasts a robust business with hundreds of employees and a business resting on a secured lending marketplace and independent home and auto lending operations.
 The company also released quarterly results for the first time, showing losses narrowing from 74.9 million Brazilian reals to 40.5 million reais in the year ago quarter.
title: 巴西贷款公司Creditas筹集了2.55亿美元，这是拉丁美洲金融科技业持续爆发的原因
title_en: Brazilian lending company Creditas raises $255 million as Latin America’s fintech explosion continues

note:
这一消息对DJI造成了巨大冲击。这家拥有14年历史的中国公司在无人机领域一直是绝对的主导力量。在美国，它占领了约77％的市场。
 长期以来，中美之间日益紧张的关系一直困扰着DJI在美国的存在，而监视能力是一个特别的关键所在。除了大获成功的消费级无人机外，DJI在工业和政府应用中也具有广泛的影响力。实际上，该公司专门提供政府产品线作为其企业产品的一部分。
  DJI是加入该列表的数十家公司之一。另外值得注意的是芯片制造商中芯国际。商务部长威尔伯·罗斯（Wilbur Ross）今天上午在新闻稿中指出该芯片制造商，他说：“我们不允许先进的美国技术来帮助建立一支日益好战的对手的军队。”
 去年，DOC尤其将华为及其几个分支机构列入名单，这一举动严重打击了这家硬件巨头。除其他外，它切断了公司对Google的Android和其他软件等美国关键技术的访问权限。华为已经选择开发自己的操作系统，但是这种登陆很难坚持下去。
 关于联邦和州政府限制使用公司技术的讨论很多，但此更新可能会更加广泛。在过去的一年中，DJI一直在为这种启示做好准备。无人机制造商在国会山花费了大量时间和资源进行游说。
 我们已与DJI和DOC取得联系，并将进行相应的更新。
note_en:
The news comes as a pretty massive blow to DJI. The 14-year-old Chinese company has been an utterly dominant force in the drone category. Here in the States, it commands an estimated 77% of the market.
 Increased tensions between the U.S. and China have long been a looming concern for DJI’s presence in the States, with surveillance capabilities being a particular sticking point. Along with their wildly successful consumer drones, DJI also has a wide reach in both industrial and governmental applications. In fact, the company specifically offers a  government line of products as part of its enterprise offerings.
  DJI is one of dozens of companies added to the list. Also of note is chipmaker, SMIC. Commerce Secretary Wilbur Ross singled the chipmaker out in a press release this morning, stating, “we will not allow advanced U.S. technology to help build the military of an increasingly belligerent adversary.”
 The DOC notably placed Huawei and several of its affiliates to the list last year, a move that has severely hamstrung the hardware giant. Among other things, it’s cut off the company’s access from key U.S. technologies like Google’s Android and other software. Huawei has opted to develop its own operating system, but the landing has been a difficult one to stick.
 There’s been a good deal of talk around restricting use of the company’s technology by federal and state departments, but this update could prove even more sweeping. DJI has been bracing for this manner of revelation over the past year and chance. The drone maker  has spent a lot of time and resources lobby on Capitol Hill.
 We’ve reached out to both DJI and the DOC and will update accordingly.
title: DJI已添加到商务部的“实体列表”中
title_en: DJI added to Commerce Department ‘entity list’

note:
这是关于我如何准备在10个月内毕业三个月的故事。
 2012年，一个学期后，我就辍学了，当时我正在学习计算机科学。我已经知道如何编程，而且我认为自己不需要学位。
 多年来，我发现自己对此抱怨很多。我看到很多机会，尤其是在国外，因为我没有所需的论文，所以这些机会是无法实现的。我觉得自己准备去读研究生，但没被录取。我不得不更加努力地向雇主证明自己。尽管我从没有发现我的知识有任何严重的差距，但我还是感到有些不足。我开始讨厌我的决定。
 2020年6月，我参加了虚拟的Slate Star Codex聚会。和往常一样，我抱怨没有学位。这是我第一次听说西方州长大学（WGU）的时候。显然，他们的在线课程没有速度限制，学生可以像通过所有考试一样快毕业。我对此表示怀疑，所以做了一些研究。
  它是经过认证的。他们的课程获得了西北大学委员会（NWCCU）的认可，该委员会是华盛顿大学（＃24）和俄勒冈大学（＃94）所使用的认可机构。
 自定进度。他们基于能力的方法使学生能够尽快掌握材料，从而完成课程。
 价格合理。他们每6个月学期的固定费用学费为3200美元，这意味着您完成学位的速度越快，您节省的资金就越多。 
第二天，我接到了招生顾问的电话。他们确认了我已阅读的所有内容，并告诉我，尽管他们没有正式接受外国学生（我是加拿大人），但他们会尝试作为例外。经过一打电话，笔录的传输/翻译/评估，英语水平证明和学费支付后，我被正式录取了他们的计算机科学理学士学位课程。
 现在，我有机会运用从Scott H. Young的MIT挑战中学到的一切，他在短短12个月内完成了MIT四年制CS课程。当然，考虑到WGU不太严格的计划以及我之前在该领域的经验，我的成就不会令人印象深刻。确实，我在10岁时写了我的第一个程序，作为软件开发人员我有多年的专业经验，甚至在大学里都学习过该课程。不过，我将在3个月内获得真正的CS学位。
  在这篇文章中，我将带领您完成整个学习过程，描述何时，何地以及如何完成该学位所需的34门课程（120个学分）。请注意，从2009年至2012年获得的前3个课程的学分（9个学分）不包括在报价的3个月和$ 5000中。 “我如何在11年内以超过5000美元的价格获得计算机科学学位”听起来并不正确。
 在查阅下表时，“ CU”代表“能力单位”，这与WGU的时间无关，等同于“学时”。 “努力”表示从初始研究到最终评估，在给定课程中投入的总时间（以小时为单位）。
  从2009年到2012年，我在CégepÉdouard-Montpetit学习了IT，并获得了大学研究文凭，大致相当于美国的副学士学位。从那里开始，学士学位通常需要再学习3年。
 除了强制参加课程外，我对该程序最大的困扰是它专注于Microsoft技术：Visual Studio，C＃，ASP.NET，SQL Server，IIS等。我是一位顽固的Linux用户，他撰写了有关邪恶的文章。高中的专有软件。尽管有最初的阻力，但我还是学到了很多东西。谁知道我以后会爱上Windows Phone，为其构建一堆应用程序（包括其第一个比特币钱包），以Xamarin开发人员的身份工作，甚至花了3年时间将WinUI移植到Android和iOS（Uno）。
 在39门课程中，我仅转移了2门课程＆＃39;相当于WGU的学分（6学分）。他们不会转移任何IT课程。他们不参加我的微积分课程，因为它已有5年以上的历史了。与可以满足WGU的所有普通教育要求的美国副学士学位不同，我的外国学位则不能。此外，在经过翻译公司（法语→英语）和国际证书评估服务（魁北克→美国）之后，有些课程的主题和水平在翻译中丢失了。如果我拥有最近（不到5岁）的副学士学位，我本来可以接受10门课程的。值得的信用。 
2012年，我在Concordia大学学习了计算机科学，并在1个学期后退学。
 第一天，我参加了2场讲座。我很快意识到，通勤2小时来慢慢听某人的PowerPoint并不是我最好的时间。讲课可能有充分的理由，但我还不够成熟，无法欣赏。相反，我自己在家阅读PowerPoint，并使用Wikipedia填补了空白。我只参加了3次校园学习，参加期中考试和期末考试。尽管我通过了全部四门课程，但我对整个大学感到很困惑，因为您花很多时间很少学习。因此，我退学了，放弃了奖学金。
 在4门课程中，我只转移了1门课程＆＃39;相当于WGU的学分（3学分）。
   在2020年7月，当我正在经历WGU的录取过程时，我决定通过在线上一些学分课程获得学分。那时，我仍然希望能够在12个月内毕业（2个学期，每个6个月）。我发现转移的学分越多，我越有可能以2个学期完成该课程。我仍然不知道1个任期就足够了。
 值得庆幸的是，在学分上在线课程时，美国学生可以使用许多负担得起的选择。这些只是提供ACE学分的课程（每门课程25至200美元）的众多网站中的少数几个：
  我选择Study.com是因为他们拥有最大的课程目录（180+），并且与WGU的转让协议最全面。他们的College Acceleration Edition计划每月收费199美元，包括2项考试，此外，您每月最多可以添加3项额外考试，每项70美元。
 他们的课程不是我选的最好的课程，但是他们可以完成工作。它们被分成几口大小的单元，每个单元都有动画讲座和强制性测验。测验的数量可能不胜枚举，如果您已经知道材料并且只想参加期末考试，我建议您去其他地方。有些课程有项目，而大多数课程都有督导的期末考试。可以使用计算机的网络摄像头进行录制，但是素材只会在7-10天后进行审核，如果您需要快速转移信用额，可能会出现问题。 
7月29日，在达到Study.com每月限制的5门课程后，我开始寻找替代方法。幸运的是，由于COVID-19，Sophia Learning已将所有课程免费开放到7月底。
 他们的课程质量优于平均水平，您甚至可以从多个讲师中进行选择。将课程分为逻辑单元，每个逻辑单元都有一组挑战（测验）和一个里程碑（单元考试）。每门课程都以一个最终的里程碑结尾，该里程碑是非监督性的（没有网络摄像头）和开放式书本，这使它们变得太容易了。
    9月1日，我终于在WGU开始了我的第一个也是唯一的一个学期，剩下23课（83学分）。
 到那时，我已经通过了9项预评估（练习考试），这与学期开始之前已经提供的课程材料不同。第一天，我在4个小时的时间内完成了4门课程。在传统学校，这需要四个月的时间。
  我低估了从以前的学校，工作，项目，书籍，论文和讲座中学到的知识。
 WGU的每个学生都被分配了一个计划导师，从第一天到毕业，他们一直为他们提供支持。每周我都会和我打个电话，我们讨论了我的进度并计划了下一周的计划。她迅速适应了我的学习进度，并确保我不会因等待答案或下一组课程获得批准而被困住。我从没想到这种人与人之间的联系会对我在WGU的经历产生如此积极的影响。还可以按需提供课程讲师，有时时间很短。
 WGU课程的质量和难度差异很大。它们来自不同的提供商，有时会使用不同的平台进行材料和评估。某些认证课程，例如Axelos ITIL 4 Foundation和Oracle Database SQL 1Z0-071，可能不属于CS计划。另一方面，将增加一个编译器课程和一个编程语言课程。 
鉴于我之前在该领域的经验，我能够跳过很多材料。对于1/5的课程，我只是忽略了材料。对于另外3/5的课程，我迅速浏览了其中。对于课程的最后1/5，我基本上阅读了整个教科书。有时，我更喜欢使用第三方资源而不是所提供的材料。幸运的是，WGU的学生社区组织了很多此类活动（技巧，笔记，抽认卡，测验，删节课本，YouTube播放列表等）。
  目标评估（OA）：通常采用单项期末考试的形式，并使用学生的网络摄像头实时进行督导。可以在任何时间（24/7）安排，甚至可以在安排前几分钟进行安排。
 绩效评估（PA）：通常采用一个或多个项目的形式，由学生提交进行评分。反馈会在1-3天内返回，使学生可以进行任何必要的修改，以适应专栏的各个方面。
 虽然我退还了一些PA进行改进，但我确保从不失败OA-即使允许重新取录（最多3-4次）。这不是一场比赛，我的目标不是要蛮力地攻读学位。当然，我是来拿纸的，但是我也以此为契机评估和弥补了我的知识空白。结果，我几乎没有通过考试。
 在WGU工作期间，我一次只专注于一门课程，并确保在开始新课程之前先完成它。我每天都取得一些进步（一个周末和生日除外），我认为这是保持动力的关键。平均而言，我每周学习40个小时，每3天完成一门课程。
 在WGU待了两个多月之后，11月3日，我提交了我的顶点项目并完成了该程序。
   从7月11日至8月3日（23天）以及从9月1日至11月3日（63天），我在Study.com，Sophia Learning和Western Governors University上在线完成了31门课程（111个学分）。加上2009年至2012年之间完成的3门大学课程（9个学分）（三个月内未计入课程，总计不超过$ 5000），我以不到$ 5000的价钱从Western Governors University获得了认可的计算机科学理学学士学位。 
总体而言，我的经历非常积极。与我在传统的实体学校学习的时间相比，这是白天和黑夜。不受任意速度限制地学习时间，地点和方式的能力非常强大。我认为将培训与测试分开是很有意义的，应该更普遍。
 由于COVID-19，我一直待在家里。我也不工作，这意味着我有很多时间分散学习时间。如果我想要的话，除了睡觉，吃饭和学习外，我什么也做不了。但是，没有速度限制，我有倦怠的危险。这些在线学校所提供的快速反馈和进步可以使学习或至少参加考试变得上瘾。
 尽管我没有严格遵守时间表，但是大多数日子看起来都是一样的。我在家庭办公室进行了大部分深度工作（练习测试，项目，试卷，考试），通常我的工作时间是上午10点至下午5点。我经常限制学习时间，在此期间我强迫自己不要做任何其他事情。当我完成一门课程时，我通常会称其为一天。否则，我只能学习大约6个小时。然后，我通常锻炼身体，洗澡，煮熟并吃晚餐。我很少在晚上学习，但是我偶尔看讲座。平均而言，我每晚睡7个半小时，比平常少一个小时。我从来没有快要筋疲力尽。
 虽然一切顺利，但我不会说这很简单。毕竟，我在这个项目上投入了600多个小时。但是我认识到，如果我以前没有接触过整个课程的话，挑战将会更大。多年的编程，观看MIT的讲座，阅读研究论文，刷新Hacker News以及浏览Wikipedia的兔子洞都使事情变得更加复杂。我确实学过新东西，并且肯定会加强一些现有知识，但是我不会说这具有变革性。大部分的工作是记住我通常会用Google搜索的内容。实际上，与计算机科学相比，我可能学到了更多关于学习的知识，但是这种经验是值得的。
 总的来说，我从来没有感到仓促，不知所措或感到压力。我的动力一直保持稳定，这归因于目标明确和挑战性的步伐。它给了我信心，并帮助我欣赏了专注的力量。我不确定是否可以在其他任何地方效仿这种经验，并且非常感谢像WGU这样的机构。
  作为一个量化的自我发烧友，我在完成本学位课程时跟踪了很多数据，包括所有投入的时间和金钱。
          如果您正在考虑获得CS的学士学位，已经在该领域有经验并且居住在美国/加拿大，则应该认真考虑Western Governors University的计算机科学理学士课程。 
如果您的品位不够严格，那么您始终可以使用自己保存的3年多来做乔治亚理工大学的计算机科学在线硕士学位（OMSCS）（低于10,000美元）。
note_en:
This is the story of how I prepared for a decade to graduate in 3 months.
 In 2012, I dropped out of college, where I was studying computer science, after just one semester. I already knew how to program and I thought I’d never need a degree.
 Over the years, I found myself complaining a lot about it. I saw many opportunities, especially abroad, that were out of reach because I didn’t have the required papers. I felt ready for graduate school but couldn’t get admitted. I had to work harder to prove myself to employers. Although I never noticed any serious gap in my knowledge, I felt that something was missing. I started to resent my decision.
 In June 2020, I attended a virtual  Slate Star Codex meetup. As usual, I complained about not having a degree. This is when I first heard about   Western Governors University (WGU). Apparently, their online programs had  no speed limit and students could graduate as quickly as they could pass all the exams. I was skeptical, so I did some research.
  It’s accredited. Their programs are accredited by the  Northwest Commission on Colleges and Universities (NWCCU), the same accreditation body used by the University of Washington (#24) and the University of Oregon (#94).
 It’s self-paced. Their competency-based approach allows students to complete courses as quickly as they can show mastery of the material.
 It’s affordable. Their flat-rate tuition of $3,200 per 6-month term means that the faster you complete the degree, the more money you save.
  The next day, I received a phone call from an enrollment counselor. They confirmed everything I had read and told me that although they didn’t officially accept foreign students (I’m Canadian), they would try to make an exception. After a dozen phone calls, transfer/translation/evaluation of transcripts, proof of English proficiency, and tuition payments, I was officially enrolled in their  Bachelor of Science in Computer Science program.
 I now had the opportunity to apply everything I had learned from  Scott H. Young’s MIT Challenge, in which he completed MIT’s 4-year CS curriculum in just 12 months. Of course, my achievement wouldn’t be as impressive, given WGU’s less rigorous program and my prior experience in the field. Indeed, I wrote my first program at 10, I had years of professional experience as a software developer, and I had even studied the subject in college. Still, I would get a real CS degree in just 3 months.
  In this post, I will take you through my entire academic journey, describing when, where, and how I completed each of the 34 courses (120 credits) required for this degree. Note that credits awarded for the first 3 courses (9 credits), taken between 2009 and 2012, were excluded from the quoted 3 months and $5000 total. “How I Got a Computer Science Degree in 11 Years for More Than $5000” didn’t sound right.
 When consulting the tables below, “CUs” stands for “Competency Units”, which is WGU’s time-agnostic equivalent to “Credit Hours”. “Effort” represents the total amount of time (in hours) invested in a given course, from initial research to final assessment.
  From 2009 to 2012, I studied IT at Cégep Édouard-Montpetit and graduated with a Diploma of College Studies, which is roughly equivalent to an Associate’s degree in the US. From there, a Bachelor’s degree typically requires an additional 3 years of study.
 My biggest gripe with the program, other than mandatory class attendance, was its focus on Microsoft technologies: Visual Studio, C#,  ASP.NET, SQL Server, IIS, etc. I was a die-hard Linux user who wrote essays about the evil of proprietary software in high school. Despite my initial resistance, I ended up learning quite a bit. Who knew I would later fall in love with Windows Phone, build a bunch of apps for it (including its first Bitcoin wallet), work as a Xamarin developer, and even spend 3 years porting  WinUI to Android and iOS ( Uno).
 Out of 39 courses, I only managed to transfer 2 courses' worth of credits (6 credits) to WGU. They wouldn’t transfer any IT course. They wouldn’t take my calculus course because it was over 5 years old. Unlike a US Associate’s degree, which would satisfy all of WGU’s general education requirements, my foreign degree wouldn’t. Also, the subject and level of some of the courses were lost in translation, after going through a translation agency (French → English) and an international credential evaluation service (Québec → USA). If I had a recent (less than 5 years old) Associate’s degree, I could have expected to receive 10 courses' worth of credits.
   In 2012, I studied computer science at Concordia University and dropped out after 1 semester.
 On my first day, I attended 2 lectures. I quickly realized that a 2-hour commute to listen to someone slowly recite a PowerPoint wasn’t the best use of my time. Lectures might exist for good reasons, but I wasn’t sophisticated enough to appreciate them. Instead, I read the PowerPoints myself, at home, and used Wikipedia to fill in the gaps. I only set foot on campus 3 more times, to take the midterm and final exams. Although I passed all 4 courses, I felt very confused about this whole university thing, where you spend a lot of time learning very little. So, I dropped out and gave up my scholarship.
 Out of 4 courses, I only managed to transfer 1 courses' worth of credits (3 credits) to WGU.
   In July 2020, as I was going through WGU’s admission process, I decided to get a head-start by taking some courses online for academic credits. At that point, I was still hoping to graduate in 12 months (2 terms of 6 months). I figured that the more credits I transferred, the more likely I would complete the program in 2 terms. I still had no idea that 1 term would be more than enough.
 Thankfully, US students have access to a lot of affordable options when it comes to taking online courses for credits. These are just a few of the many websites that offer courses with  ACE credits in the $25-$200 per course range:
  I chose  Study.com because they had the largest course catalog (180+) and the most comprehensive transfer agreements with WGU. Their College Acceleration Edition plan costs $199/month and includes 2 exams, on top of which you can add up to 3 additional exams per month for $70 each.
 Their courses are not the best I’ve taken, but they get the job done. They’re split into bite-size units, each with animated lectures and mandatory quizzes. The number of quizzes can be overwhelming, and I recommend looking elsewhere if you already know the material and just want to take the final exam. Some courses have projects, and most courses have a proctored final exam. Proctoring is done using your computer’s webcam, but the footage is only reviewed 7-10 days later, which can be a problem if you need to transfer credits quickly.
    On July 29 th, after reaching  Study.com’s monthly 5-course limit, I started looking for alternatives. Luckily,  Sophia Learning had made all their courses free until the end of July due to COVID-19.
 The quality of their courses is better than average, and you can even choose from multiple lecturers. Courses are broken down into logical units, each with a set of challenges (quizzes) and a milestone (unit exam). Each course ends with a final milestone which is non-proctored (no webcam) and open-book, making them almost too easy.
    On September 1 st, I finally started my first and only term at WGU, with 23 courses (83 credits) left to go.
 By then, I had already passed 9 pre-assessments (practice exams), which unlike course material were already available before the start of the term. On my first day, I completed 4 courses in the span of 4 hours. At a traditional school, it would have taken 4 months.
  I had underestimated how much knowledge I had gained from previous schools, jobs, projects, books, papers, and talks.
 Each student at WGU gets assigned a program mentor, who supports them from their first day through graduation. I had a phone call with mine every week, in which we discussed my progress and planned the week ahead. She quickly adapted to my pace of study and made sure I never got stuck waiting for an answer or for the next set of courses to be approved. I never expected that this human point of contact would have such a positive impact on my experience at WGU. Course instructors were also available on-demand, sometimes on surprisingly short notice.
 The quality and difficulty of courses at WGU varies a lot. They’re sourced from different providers, sometimes using different platforms for material and assessments. Some certification courses, such as Axelos ITIL 4 Foundation and Oracle Database SQL 1Z0-071, probably don’t belong in a CS program. On the other hand, a compilers course and a programming languages course would have been welcome additions.
 Given my prior experience in the field, I was able to skip a lot of material. For 1/5 of courses, I simply ignored the material. For another 3/5 of courses, I quickly skimmed through it. For the last 1/5 of courses, I basically read the whole text book. Sometimes, I prefered third-party resources over the provided material. Fortunately, WGU’s student community curates a lot of them (tips, notes, flashcards, quizzes, abridged text books, YouTube playlists, etc.)
  Objective Assessment (OA): Usually takes the form of a single final exam, proctored in real-time using the student’s webcam. Can be scheduled at any time (24/7), even minutes before taking place.
 Performance Assessment (PA): Usually takes the form of one or more projects, submitted by the student for grading. Feedback is returned within 1-3 days, allowing the student to make any required modifications to satisfy all aspects of the rubric.
 While I had some PAs returned for improvements, I made sure to never fail an OA – even if retakes (up to 3-4) were allowed. It wasn’t a race, and my goal wasn’t to brute force my way into a degree. I came for the piece of paper, sure, but I also used it as an opportunity to assess and fill gaps in my knowledge. As a result, I never came close to failing an exam.
 During my time at WGU, I focused on a single course at a time and made sure to complete it before starting a new one. I made some progress every single day (except for one weekend and on my birthday), which I believe was key to maintaining momentum. On average, I studied for 40 hours a week and completed a course every 3 days.
 On November 3 rd, after just over 2 months at WGU, I submitted my capstone project and completed the program.
   From July 11 th to August 3 rd (23 days) and from September 1 st to November 3 rd (63 days), I completed 31 courses (111 credits) online across  Study.com,  Sophia Learning, and  Western Governors University. Combined with 3 college courses (9 credits) completed between 2009 and 2012 (not accounted for in the 3 months and $5000 total), I received an accredited Bachelor of Science in Computer Science degree from Western Governors University for less than $5000.
  My experience was very positive overall. Compared to my time at traditional brick and mortar schools, it was night and day. The ability to study when, where, and how you want, without arbitrary speed limits, is extremely empowering. I believe that separating training from testing makes a lot of sense and should be more common.
 Due to COVID-19, I stayed home the entire time. I wasn’t working either, which meant that I had plenty of undistracted time to study. If I had wanted, I could have done nothing but sleep, eat, and study. However, without a speed limit, I was at risk of burning out. The rapid feedback and progress enabled by these online schools can make learning, or at least taking tests, quite addictive.
 Although I didn’t stick to any strict schedule, most days looked the same. I did most of my deep work (practice tests, projects, papers, exams) in my home office, which I usually occupied from 10 AM to 5 PM. I often time-blocked study sessions, during which I forced myself not to do anything else. When I finished a course, I usually called it a day. Otherwise, about 6 hours study was all I could take. Then, I usually exercised, showered, cooked, and ate dinner. I rarely studied in the evening, but I occassionally watched lectures. On average, I slept 7 and a half hours a night, which is an hour less than usual. I never came close to burning out.
 While everything went smoothly, I wouldn’t say it was easy. After all, I invested over 600 hours in this project. But I recognize that it would have been a lot more challenging if I hadn’t been exposed to pretty much the entire curriculum before. Years of programming, watching MIT lectures, reading research papers, refreshing Hacker News, and going down Wikipedia rabbit holes all added up to something. I did learn new things, and it definitely reinforced some existing knowledge, but I wouldn’t say it was transformative. The bulk of the effort was memorizing things that I normally would have Googled. In fact, I might have learned more about learning than about computer science, but the experience was worth it.
 Overall, I never felt rushed, overwhelmed, or stressed. My motivation remained stable the entire time, which I attribute to clear goals and a challenging pace. It gave me confidence and helped me appreciate the power of focus. I’m not sure I could have emulated this experience anywhere else, and I’m very grateful that institutions like WGU exist.
  As a  quantified self enthusiast, I tracked a lot of data while completing this degree, including all invested time and money.
          If you’re thinking about getting a Bachelor’s degree in CS, already have experience in the field, and live in the US/Canada, you should seriously consider  Western Governors University’s Bachelor of Science in Computer Science program.
 If it’s not prestigious or rigorous enough for your taste, you can always use the 3+ years you’ve saved to do an  Online Master of Science in Computer Science (OMSCS) at Georgia Tech (<$10,000).
title: 我在不到3个月的时间内获得了计算机科学学位的费用低于$ 5000
title_en: I Got a Computer Science Degree in 3 Months for Less Than $5000

note:
12月9日，唐纳德·特朗普（Donald Trump）发表了一条不正确的推文，但至少比他自从竞选连任以来发表的大部分言论更接近真相。
  “晚上十点特朗普写道，在选举之夜，我们以97％的优势赢得了所谓的“ bookies”。所谓的“ bookies”从来没有让特朗普成为97％的最爱，但在11月3日深夜，许多在线体育博彩确实的确支持他赢得总统职位。在晚上10点之间晚上11:30东部，其中许多庄家（都在海外，因为在美国选举选举不合法）都发布了赔率，使特朗普有大约70％的获胜机会。下午10:30，总部位于哥斯达黎加的博瓦达（Bovada）是美国赌徒最喜欢的离岸股票之一，特朗普的赔率是-775，这意味着成功的$ 775赌注将返还$ 100的利润。这意味着特朗普获胜的可能性为89％。
        如果您在选举之夜被诅咒到关注博彩市场，那么这些数字可能会像《纽约时报》 2016年关于大类固醇的针一样打击您。政治分析家警告说，延迟邮寄选票的计数可能会造成“红色海市rage楼”，根据民主党选举前的大选日投票，共和党人看起来会不错。但是下注几率并不能买到那种种族理论。当特朗普显然会赢得佛罗里达大选并在北卡罗来纳州，俄亥俄州和德克萨斯州看上去不错时，他们向特朗普努力前进。
  博彩市场并不是很好的预测指标，但并没有成为现实。在选举中下注的在线博彩公司看到了有史以来最大的单项意外收益。要了解原因，您需要了解选举博彩和唐纳德·特朗普的支持者。
      离岸体育博彩没有分享详细的财务数据，但至少在美国历史上，2020年选举似乎是最大的在线博彩活动，更不用说欧洲持牌博彩公司可能做出的成就了。
  Bovada的主要赔率创造者Pat Morrow说，他的网站处理了“八位数”的选举下注，其中95％来自美国人，与该网站上一次最大的赛事酋长相比，这场比赛下注了30％的现金。 2月的49ers超级碗。根据体育博彩品牌经理戴夫·梅森（Dave Mason）的说法，在总部位于巴拿马的BetOnline，此次选举也带来了历史上最大的总赌注：“八位数”。
  全球在线博彩业是赚几千万，几亿还是数十亿美元的选举赌注，可能永远不会知道。但是要想出一个主意，想象一下在最大的体育赛事上要投入多少钱，然后再提高多少。 
梅森说：“每个人都知道，超级碗是迄今为止本年度最大的赌注赛事，是最后两场比赛的两倍。” “这绝对是疯了。不仅有很多人下注，而且还有很多下注。双方都有五位数甚至六位数的赌注。我们从未见过这样的东西。”
      选举之夜的赔率转移大富翁，以及所有的钱都在网上，这并不意味着赔率制造者知道主流媒体所不知道的东西。取而代之的是，特朗普的飙升是一种现象的高峰，这种现象一年四季都在发生。许多特朗普的支持者都确信他不会输，他们花了很多钱赌他，以至于扭曲了市场（对他（最终是体育博彩）有利）。
    “这是我所见过的最非理性的市场，”佛罗里达州坦帕市的赌博作家Collin Sherwin说，他为Vox Media的DraftKings Nation报道了整个行业。
  在2020年初，特朗普在体育博彩中一直是稳定的，甚至是压倒性的热门。有道理；竞选活动尚未认真开始，但现任领导人通常会获胜，“经济”状况良好。
    2月27日，Bovada让特朗普以-180获胜。也就是说，下注$ 180会返还$ 100的利润，这意味着他获胜的机会为64％。拜登在民主党初选中挣扎，是+2000，这意味着$ 100的选择将利润返回$ 2,000，如果他赢得大选。但是随后，拜登-比起主要竞争对手伯尼·桑德斯（Bernie Sanders）更倾向于将胜利者描绘成更可能的胜利者-开始反弹。
    1）世界上大部分地区都看到了特朗普对病毒的灾难性处理，而他的认可率下降了，因此他的下注几率变得更长。
  2）人们被困在里面，花钱的地方更少了。就像游戏化的投资应用程式Robinhood在大流行初期，用户的大量增长一样，选举博彩市场也是如此。 
3）大流行使人们没有可喜的事件。莫罗说：“虽然似乎很少发生，但世界上仍在发生的少数事情之一是，初选仍在进行中。”
  随着大流行的蔓延，特朗普对管理大流行的不愿变得明显，拜登的可能性越来越大。 6月4日，拜登在波瓦达（Bovada）首次成为人们的最爱，当特朗普还没赚钱时，他就升至-110。
        在所有这些期间，特朗普的几率比选举关键人士建议的提前数英里。 6月初，投注赔率表示，拜登的获胜比掷硬币更容易。同时，FiveThirtyEight基于民意测验的模型使拜登赢得了大约70％的选票。 （内特·西尔弗（Nate Silver）反复指出了这种差异，并在8月表示，博彩市场“变得很愚蠢，以至于在这一点上可能成为逆势指标。”）《经济学人》的模型将拜登的比例接近80％。
  无论您现在是否相信投票和选举模型的优点，这都是一个奇怪的分歧。当赔率制定者决定参加十大足球比赛时，分析模型会在很大程度上说明积分的差距。如果俄亥俄州立大学以43分的优势击败罗格斯大学，那是因为一台计算机预测了该社区的结果。然后，点差会根据受伤情况，下注方式以及赔率制定者收到的任何有用信息而有所变化。
      在这里，计算机和投注赔率保持很大的差距。赔率制造者不会根据他们认为会发生的事情设定赔率。他们旨在限制风险，并创造“房子”（庄家）获利的最佳机会。一方赌钱太多是一种责任。在这种情况下，一方面有很多钱：特朗普的。
  “我们需要拜登很大，” BetOnline的梅森说。 “我们需要他很大。”
  莫罗说，全年在波瓦达（Bovada）的收入对特朗普来说是2比1左右。在BetOnline，大约60％的投注者在特朗普身上，尽管总奖金接近50-50，这意味着拜登的支持者下了一些大赌注。 
在一年中的大部分时间里，特朗普的短期获胜几率（要求投注者为获得较小的奖金而冒险花更多的钱），并不反映内部政治知识，也不反映出赔率制造者是超级英雄。博彩公司在特朗普下了很多赌注，以至于他们一直试图劝阻人们不要再押注他了。
  梅森说：“特朗普伙计们全力以赴。” “按照真实的赔率，他们可能可以得到更好的价格，但是我们以及可能其他所有书籍都将价格保持在低于应有的水平，这仅仅是因为我们对此非常了解。”
          有一些因素可以解释特朗普一年来的赌注激增。首先，考虑定期从事在线博彩的中位数人。突然出现在您的大脑中的刻板印象可能是正确的。
    “特朗普的支持者很大声，”莫罗说。 “他们以不爱大多数候选人的方式爱特朗普，但他们也代表了许多体育博彩的人群。这些人年龄在18至45岁之间，通常是白人男性。”
  此外，考虑到特朗普坚持认为他至少在法律上不会输掉这次选举。他表示，如果他输了，他将依靠共和党下属和法官来推翻结果。 （然后他进行了倾斜，如果不是翻转的话。）即使到12月，只有四分之一的共和党人相信拜登的胜利是合法的。在选举学院投票赞成拜登的第二天，星期二，人们仍然在预测市场PredictIt上支持特朗普，这意味着任何想赚钱的人都可以押注拜登。
      “如果您在[大选前后]观看OANN，就会发现自己和我永远都不会认识的事物，”舍温说。 “这只是一个完全不同的世界。而且，生活在泡沫中的老年人，有钱人和白人中有足够的钱来利用此下注机会，原因有两个。其一，他们的感受和所相信的事物，但二，这也是一种将其坚持到“流媒体”的方式。您知道我的意思吗？我认为他们可能像，哦，你们不知道您在说什么，我认为我可以从中获利，因为您不了解Real America。”
    宣威关于有钱的较老投注者的理论与莫罗关于投注者大多是年轻人的观点相矛盾。但是两个人都同意，MAGA的言论影响了博彩市场的行为。 
这是特朗普的个性崇拜激发了大量非理性博彩的另一个线索。在大选的前几个月，博瓦达的博彩者可以将钱投入两个不同的大选市场。一个是“拜登反对特朗普”，另一个是“民主党反对共和党”。
  尽管特朗普获得了对拜登的绝大部分资金，但那些押注一个政党的人却向民主党投了更多钱。
      “人们想要一张特朗普（博彩）票，”莫罗说。 “他们不想拥有'共和党赢得总统选举的门票。”
    “人们押注他们最喜欢的球队，对吗？”梅森补充。 “如果您是洋基队的球迷，那么您每晚都在赌荡荡的洋基队。他们很棒，这很有帮助，但是那里有很多偏爱。您也会在政治博彩中看到这一点。你真的是您会看到人们用心打赌。”
  在选举日，FiveThirtyEight的模型为拜登赢得了89％的获胜机会。另一方面，博彩市场通常将拜登排在60％至70％之间。
  通常，将一方描述为获胜的可能性比他们应该获得的要多（如果获胜，则降低派息）会向另一方向发送赌注。但是对于特朗普球迷来说，下注几率似乎可以“证明”民意测验是错误的，聪明的人知道这一点，他们的人将获胜。
      “也许我们正在向特朗普的支持者发出信号，‘嘿，保持信念，这比民意测验所暗示的要近得多，”莫罗说。 “但是我们真正要做的只是对债务做出反应。” 
随着选举之夜的进行，即使是那些从事政治活动的自由主义者看到佛罗里达州和北卡罗来纳州的回归，也对2016年类似的灾难感到相当担忧。那天晚上，特朗普对特朗普的赔率很大。当时，特朗普在许多网站上花了一个或两个小时，占70％或以上，这是最好的选择。赔率制造者正在考虑自己对特朗普的责任，但也要实时做出反应，拜登会随身携带密歇根州，威斯康星州和宾夕法尼亚州还不是既成事实。
    梅森说：``有人在观看CNN或Fox上的红色和蓝色地图或我们正在观看的任何东西时出现过déjàvu的事，而早期的统计数据表明所有这些摇摆状态都归特朗普所有。''
  当拜登的赔率变得足够长时，一些投注者支持了拜登，他们获得了可观的利润。但莫罗说，受到实时投注限制的拜登钱几乎没有削弱博瓦达的财务状况。这笔钱仍然有利于特朗普。
      “我的意思是，让拜登活着的任何人，都很好，”莫罗说，“但这丝毫没有伤害我们。这甚至不是肉伤。我们仍然在清理。”
  那天深夜，福克斯新闻称拜登为亚利桑那州，而内华达州也开始对他有利。一夜之间，新一批的邮件投票使拜登在威斯康星州和密歇根州显然是最受欢迎的，并暗示他在宾夕法尼亚州的步伐良好。到了早上，拜登已成为整个市场上的重头戏。下午2时30分投票后两天，星期四东部，拜登在波瓦达的投票率为-1100，特朗普为+575。
    莫罗说，从头到尾，他在自己网站上的大部分赌注仍留在特朗普身上，包括大选后特朗普的“ 3比4或1比4”。
    “我认为部分原因是，'他可能会以这种速度失去传统选举，但我们仍然处于困境之中：这可能会通过立法机关或法院，'如果您选择唐纳德·特朗普，这同样会再次出现。说真的，这是他一直说要走的路。”莫罗说。 
多数庄家花了数周时间才决定对大选下注。梅森（Mason）和莫罗（Morrow）经常听到拜登（Biden）投注者焦急地等待着他们的赔付，而特朗普的投注者感谢他们不急于做出仓促的决定。当他们最终解决州和国家的赌注，等到有争议的州认证结果时，大型海上体育博彩的公众面孔得到了特朗普投注者的不同回应。
    “‘你会后悔的。’”莫罗回忆起愤怒的特朗普投注者的来信。 ‘‘你会发怒的。这将是你的尽头。您将在麦当劳工作。’”（莫罗与安提瓜的赔率相差无几，那里似乎至少有一个麦当劳。）
  有时很难说出哪些所谓的特朗普赌徒是真实的，哪些不是。一个屏幕截图显示一个投注者声称已向特朗普下注27,000美元并要求退款，但愿意让他一半的赌注作为妥协退还给他。
        舍温说：“我们在政治上使人们生活在两个不同的现实中，并且人们在两个不同的现实中博彩。”
    庄家们是完全无辜的吗？他们是否一直在诱骗特朗普的支持者，为他们愿意下注的所有赌徒挤牛奶？
  不是一开始。赔率制定者与我们其他人一样，生活在相同的模拟中，无法使特朗普的支持者停止下注自己的英雄（并停止增加体育博彩的责任）。 Bovada和BetOnline都对特朗普的赔率感到保守，试图放慢他的支持者的速度。
  “如果有的话，我们正在诱捕拜登的赌徒，”梅森说。 “我们给他们折扣。” 
只是在选举之后，庄家才达到诉讼程序的“从婴儿那里拿糖果”部分。梅森在选举后的第二天发了推文，当时特朗普在BetOnline的赔率是+525，而80％的赌注仍在特朗普身上，“我们的巨大责任在继续增加。”
    对我来说，这听起来像是在诱骗那个傻瓜。但即使如此：特朗普的支持者还是自己做。如果有人没有意识到商人渴望将客户从他们的钱中分离出来，这不是体育博彩公司的错。
  这些体育博彩在政治运动拒绝接受现实中赢得了数百万的回报。这不是赔率制定者计划以如此大的优势获胜，尽管这样做使特朗普成为相对偏爱的人，但这样做仍然是一大笔好运。
  2020年的选举有许多失败者：特朗普，他的支持者和美国民主领袖。但是几乎是偶然的，这场比赛创造了超越乔·拜登的巨大胜利者。最大的，可以说，是基于没有在华盛顿，但在像巴拿马市的地方，由政府拜登当选领导完全不受调控。
